serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of steamed vegetarian dumplings is approximately 4 pieces, weighing around 120 g, as commonly served at hawker centres and dim sum restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: 素菜蒸饺 Steamed Vegetarian Dumplings — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo database for 素菜蒸饺 Steamed Vegetarian Dumplings (cr_id G00010045). All per-100g macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — steamed wheat-based dumplings / jiaozi analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for steamed vegetarian dumplings with spinach skin found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is estimated by analogy to steamed wheat-flour dumplings (jiaozi) with vegetable filling, which are documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care) as low-GI foods in the range 45–52. The vegetable-dominant filling (tofu, mushrooms, leafy greens, vermicelli) and steaming method further support a low-GI classification. GL per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 48 × 8.03 × 1.2 / 100 ≈ 3.8 g per 120 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium at 46 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: With only 46 mg sodium per 100 g, steamed vegetarian dumplings are an excellent low-sodium choice for hypertensive individuals; however, patients should avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as regular soy sauce or chilli sauce, which can substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~48), low carbohydrate (8 g per 100 g), negligible sugar (0.21 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low glycaemic index (~48), modest carbohydrate content (8 g per 100 g), and high protein from tofu (8.71 g per 100 g) collectively support a moderate post-meal glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to 4–5 pieces and avoid sweet dipping sauces to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.8 g per 100 g), no deep-frying, plant-based filling
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The steaming preparation method, low saturated fat content (~0.8 g per 100 g), plant-based protein from tofu, and absence of red meat make steamed vegetarian dumplings a heart-friendly option; patients with cardiovascular disease should pair with a low-sodium dipping sauce and monitor overall portion size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients: mushrooms (beta-glucans), bok choy (sulforaphane), negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mushrooms and cruciferous vegetables in the filling provide beta-glucans and sulforaphane with anti-inflammatory properties; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should use low-sodium soy sauce for dipping to avoid sodium-driven fluid retention and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Vegetarian Dumplings (素菜蒸饺, Sù cài zhēng jiǎo) are a popular Chinese-origin dim sum dish widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and dim sum restaurants across Singapore. The dumplings feature a distinctive green spinach-infused wheat flour wrapper that is thin, slightly chewy, and translucent when steamed. The savoury filling combines black fungus (wood ear mushroom), shiitake or button mushrooms, glass vermicelli noodles, firm beancurd (tofu) cubes, xiao bai cai (baby bok choy), and shredded turnips, seasoned lightly with soy sauce and sesame oil. The steaming method preserves the natural flavours and moisture of the vegetables without added oil, resulting in a light, clean taste profile. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 101 kcal, 8.71 g protein (primarily from tofu), 3.78 g fat (low saturated fat ~0.8 g), and 8.03 g carbohydrate with negligible sugar (0.21 g). Sodium is very low at 46 mg per 100 g, making this dish suitable for sodium-restricted diets. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 48 (low GI), supporting blood glucose management. The dish is fully vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension (very low sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, moderate carbohydrate, high plant protein), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, plant-based, no deep-frying). Commonly served as a lunch or dinner item, typically 4 pieces per serving (~120 g).

confidence_reason: Exact match found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for 素菜蒸饺 Steamed Vegetarian Dumplings (cr_id G00010045) with a source confidence of 0.95. All core macronutrients and key micronutrients are directly sourced from this canonical HPB record. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed wheat-based vegetable dumplings, clearly documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: steamed preparation with plant-based filling; no hydrogenated fats used; confirmed negligible per HPB source context. 3. dietary_fibre_g of 1.50 g per 100 g is estimated from the vegetable and tofu filling composition (black fungus, bok choy, mushrooms, turnip) using USDA FoodData Central component data as a supplementary reference, as HPB does not report fibre separately for this item. 4. saturated_fat_g of 0.80 g per 100 g is estimated from tofu and vegetable fat composition; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item. 5. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database for this specific dish as of 2026-04-16. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on steamed wheat-flour vegetable dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific spinach-skin formulation was found. 7. Cholesterol of 28 mg per 100 g is reported by HPB; this is plausible given the tofu content (tofu contains trace cholesterol in some preparations) and is retained as sourced. 8. The dish is classified as vegan-suitable based on the described ingredients; restaurant preparations may vary.
